text: In our country, reservoir inundation disasters frequently occur, causing substantial economic losses. For example, the Yanwang Bizi Reservoir in Liaoning Province has a designed normal storage water level of 213.5m, completed in June 2000. In January of the following year, when the storage elevation reached 209.5m, several villages along the reservoir's banks experienced phenomena such as cracked houses and wall subsidence, while wells and vegetable cellars near the water surface even showed signs of underwater collapse. The Guanting Reservoir in Hebei Province faced inundation disasters due to inadequate attention to the reservoir environment during its early development, leading to marshland formation and house collapses along the riverbanks after water filling, subsequently resulting in reduced or total crop failures and mass death of fruit trees. The Ganlian Dam Hydropower Station in Yunnan, after reservoir filling, caused backflow to the Jinghong Basin, resulting in inundation in certain areas and threatening surrounding buildings, crops, and economic forest species like rubber and fruit trees. Since the impoundment of the Songhua River Dadingzi Mountain navigation hub project in 2007, the reservoir's normal storage water level has been significantly higher than during the dry season. The unique topography, geography, good permeability of the aquifer, shallow groundwater, low terrain, and close hydraulic connection with the groundwater on both banks have led to frequent inundation phenomena, adversely affecting crops, buildings, roads, and even residents' daily lives. The Shifo Temple Reservoir's main body began construction at the end of May 2003 and took two and a half years to complete. The reservoir started trial filling in 2006, and when the water level rose to 46.4m, significant external seepage was detected, resulting in inundation phenomena. Due to the low-lying terrain in the downstream areas of the right bank auxiliary dam, such as Zhujia Fort and Chenping Fort, severe inundation disasters occurred in this region, causing significant harm to arable land, forest land, and other agricultural production, greatly impacting residents' normal production, life, and farming activities. A hydropower station project in western Xinjiang did not consider the elevated groundwater levels at the reservoir's tail during its design phase, resulting in an underestimation of the inundation area. After the reservoir started filling, it was discovered that residents' homes experienced damp floors, wall cracks, and flooded cellars.

The Ganjiang Estuary Comprehensive Rehabilitation Project is a significant integrated management initiative implemented in Nanchang City, Jiangxi Province, China. It represents an essential water conservancy project aimed at improving the ecological environment, enhancing water quality, reducing water pollution, and preventing and controlling flood disasters in the downstream region of the Ganjiang River. The estuary area of the Ganjiang River is a crucial economic and cultural center in Nanchang City. The execution of this project will contribute to the improvement of water environment quality and the health of the hydrological ecological system in the region, as well as enhance local economic development and social stability.

This study focuses on a specific submerged area within the Ganjiang Estuary, investigating the inundation effects of a plain-type inter-river reservoir. Initially, both indoor data collection and outdoor field surveys were conducted to gather a series of information regarding hydrological geology, engineering geology, and meteorological hydrology in the study area. Based on this data, the stratigraphic structure, aquifer structure, boundary conditions, and other parameters of the study area were determined, and a conceptual model of hydrological geology was built. A three-dimensional geological model of the study area was constructed using borehole data combined with GMS software to analyze the stratigraphic structure, while groundwater level monitoring data, hydrometeorological data, and hydrological geological parameters were utilized for model calibration and verification alongside field investigations and tests. 

To explore the inundation impacts of plain-type inter-river reservoirs and provide scientific support for studies in these areas, a numerical simulation model was established for predicting groundwater levels and inundation extents. Depending on the actual conditions within the study area, numerical simulations were conducted to analyze the effects of varying stratigraphic structures, different surface water levels, and dam heights on water level changes within the Ganjiang Estuary study area. The following research conclusions were obtained: (1) When the local surface water level in the study area reaches 13.116m, approximately 15% of the area will be affected by inundation disasters, with severely inundated areas accounting for 3% and lightly inundated areas for 12% of the total area. At a water level of 15.794m, severely inundated areas will represent 59% and lightly inundated areas 21%. As the water level continues to rise to 17.362m, only 4% of the region will remain unaffected by inundation disasters, while severely affected areas will constitute 87%, covering approximately 87.970 km². (2) Following the completion of the Ganjiang Estuary Comprehensive Rehabilitation Project, if the reservoir’s water level reaches above 19.794m, over 94.403% of the region will fall into severely inundated areas, with only approximately 1.291 km² remaining unaffected. (3) Three different stratigraphic profiles were taken from the study area, employing a confined aquifer model to calculate the thickness variations of aquifers during water level changes. The calculated results show that the thickness of the saturated zone in profile BTK404–BTK408 ranges from 3.94 to 4.03m, while in profile BTK412–BTK414 it ranges from 4.96 to 5.04m, and in profile BTK418–BTK420 from 7.37 to 7.58m. The results indicate that the calculations from the confined aquifer model are in good accord with the numerical simulation results.

Reservoirs serve functions such as flood control, power generation, irrigation, water supply, and aquaculture. However, once a reservoir is officially in operation, with the continuous rise of the water level, the groundwater level becomes elevated due to pressure, and inundation hazards around the reservoir become prominent. Due to factors such as soil physical properties and changes in groundwater levels, vegetation growth in the inundated areas will be adversely affected, leading to wilting and even death, while the foundations of buildings may also experience damage. This study focuses on the inundated areas surrounding the Shifo Temple Reservoir, providing a comprehensive analysis of the mechanisms behind the inundation and preliminarily delineating the inundation boundaries. Utilizing groundwater simulation software, a numerical model of the groundwater in the study area was established to predict groundwater level changes at different storage levels. Through the established inundation hazard assessment system, and combined with a gray clustering evaluation model, a comprehensive assessment of inundation hazards in the study area was performed. This system incorporated various assessment indicators, enhancing the objectivity of the results and providing a reasonable basis for future management of inundated areas. The main research conclusions are as follows: (1) By observing the water level changes of the Lama River using a water gauge, and correlating the reservoir water level with the river channel water level, it is evident that the backflow phenomenon generated by the reservoir’s water storage significantly affects the water level of the Lama River, serving as one cause of inundation. Soil samples from both banks of the river were analyzed for changes in soil moisture content, indicating that topography affects the degree of inundation. The analysis of the correlation between groundwater levels in the inundated areas and their influencing factors showed the order of significance as follows: storage level > air temperature > precipitation > evaporation > wind speed. This analysis of the current inundation situation in the study area can serve as a reference for establishing a reservoir inundation assessment system and evaluation. (2) Using three-dimensional groundwater simulation software, a 3D geological model of the study area was constructed with borehole data extracted from the engineering geological profile of the Shifo Temple Reservoir and virtual borehole data generated via interpolation. After gridding the model and importing soil parameter data, a numerical model of the groundwater was created. Following verification and correction, this model was used to calculate groundwater levels in inundated areas at various reservoir storage levels. According to the inundation assessment criteria, when the storage level is at a normal capacity of 46.2m, the extent of severe inundation in the study area exceeds 85% of the total area, indicating serious inundation hazards. The computed water level data can serve as parameter information for comprehensive evaluation of the inundated area. (3) Addressing the issue of singular factors in inundation assessment, this study considers multiple factors affecting crop growth (such as moisture content, natural porosity, saturation, etc.) as assessment indicators, establishing quantitative standards for each factor. Using the Analytic Hierarchy Process, an inundation assessment index system was developed, combining the gray evaluation method based on the whitening weight function for assessment of the inundation hazard level in the inundated area. The evaluation results indicate severe inundation hazards in the region, making the soil unsuitable for crop cultivation. The assessment findings can provide a basis for reasonable compensation and management of the inundated areas.

Reservoir inundation is a comprehensive disaster caused by the rise of surface water levels due to reservoir storage or other human activities, which leads to elevated shallow groundwater levels, triggering various issues. The coupling of low-temperature conditions in cold regions complicates the already intricate inundation problems in terms of mechanisms, impacts, and manifestations. 

This study focuses on the upper inundation range of the Daqingzi Mountain navigation hub project, a typical area characterized by cold region inundation, analyzing and investigating the inundation features of a large typical area. This helps identify the factors influencing reservoir inundation in cold regions. A qualitative analysis of conventional influence factors (groundwater level, soil texture, capillary rise height, surface vegetation, and structures/safety elevation) and cold region-specific factors (snowmelt and freeze-thaw processes) is conducted to study their influencing mechanisms.

Subsequently, a quantitative numerical simulation study on the impact of reservoir inundation in a small-scale calculation area is carried out. After analyzing the boundary, structure, function, input, and output of the aquifer in the calculation area, a hydrogeological conceptual model is developed using groundwater simulation software GMS, which is then converted into a numerical simulation model for identification and validation. 

Based on this, combined with inundation discrimination and evaluation methods, a reservoir inundation evaluation model is constructed, which includes three equations: groundwater flow dynamics, reservoir inundation impact assessment, and coupling equations. The impact mechanisms of each influencing factor are embedded into the simulation model to reflect their variations.

The developed model is applied in two ways: ① From the perspective of exacerbating inundation, a simulation evaluation is performed using a reference group and five preset schemes changing the influencing factors, revealing the influence patterns of various factors on reservoir inundation within a certain range; ② From the perspective of managing inundation, changing the influencing factors is regarded as a preset management scheme, and the effectiveness and feasibility of the management measures are simulated and evaluated.

Results indicate that among the three conventional factors influencing reservoir inundation, an increase in groundwater level under the same variation has a more pronounced impact on inundation than capillary rise height or safety elevation. For cold region-specific factors, snowmelt significantly impacts the short-term conditions of the inundation zone, while the freezing period of the freeze-thaw process has a pronounced effect on inundation; however, the thawing period does not exacerbate the inundation significantly. In terms of management schemes, both lowering the groundwater level and decreasing capillary rise height or safety elevation theoretically yield satisfactory results, but practically only reducing groundwater level offers relatively higher feasibility for large-scale management.

Following this study, several extension issues are briefly discussed, including the overall inundation range of the Songhua River upstream of the Daqingzi Mountain Reservoir and an overview of reservoir inundation in the Heilong River Basin, along with an evaluation of the advantages and disadvantages of the methods based on field measurements and numerical simulation used in this research.

Reservoir inundation is a phenomenon caused by the rise of shallow groundwater levels due to reservoir water storage or various anthropogenic activities, leading to a range of hazards. Inundation issues often directly impact the determination of normal reservoir water levels and can even affect the selection of dam sites. The occurrence of reservoir inundation results from the interplay of multiple factors, primarily involving the topography and geomorphology of reservoir banks, lithology and structure, hydrogeological conditions, water chemistry, hydrometeorology, the operational modes of reservoirs, and the influence of human activities. It also encompasses multiple disciplines, including engineering geology, hydrogeology, environmental geology, and hydraulic structures. Reservoirs located in plain and hilly areas, characterized by relatively flat terrain and widespread Quaternary loose sediments, are often most susceptible to inundation, with the broadest impact. In mountainous reservoir areas, the presence of mineral deposits, underground structures, and national defense projects necessitates preventive measures against inundation. Reservoirs in karst regions carry the risk of leakage to external areas due to the existence of solution channels. Once reservoir inundation occurs, it frequently results in the marshland and salinization of large areas of fertile land near the reservoir edges, reduces the foundation strength of industrial and civil buildings (with significant impacts on loess and expansive soil foundations), or causes flooding of mining pits, thereby affecting mineral extraction.

In the collected academic papers and monographs, the concept of reservoir inundation can be summarized into the following three interpretations. (1) From the perspective of engineering geology: After the reservoir is filled, the groundwater level in the surrounding area rises, approaching or exceeding the ground surface, resulting in marshland formation, salinization, and deterioration of foundation conditions for structures in the vicinity of the reservoir. This phenomenon is referred to as impoundment inundation; the inundation of mines and underground structures due to seepage from the reservoir, along with the deterioration of agricultural, residential, and transportation conditions in the downstream and adjacent valley lowlands, is termed seepage inundation. Both impoundment inundation and seepage inundation are collectively referred to as reservoir inundation. (2) From the perspective of hydraulic and hydropower engineering: After the reservoir is filled, the corresponding rise in groundwater nearby approaches or exceeds the ground level, leading to salinization of farmland, marsh development, deterioration of foundation conditions for structures, and inflow into mines; this phenomenon is known as reservoir inundation. (3) From the perspective of hydrogeology and environmental geology: Reservoir inundation is an environmental hydrogeological issue concerning reservoirs. It refers to the situation where the reservoir induces an upward pressure on the groundwater, causing the original groundwater to be obstructed from draining due to the increased reservoir water level and the elevated river channel levels, leading to rising groundwater levels around the reservoir basin and both banks of the inflowing river, saturation of rock and soil, soil salinization, marsh formation; inundation of mine pits by industrial and mining enterprises; and deterioration of foundation conditions for structures (houses, bridges, railways, roads), resulting in hazardous conditions for agricultural production and residents' lives upstream of the reservoir. All three concepts indicate that the fundamental phenomenon of reservoir inundation is the rise in groundwater levels caused by the increase in reservoir water levels and emphasize the various geological and environmental hazards that may be triggered by reservoir inundation. The concept proposed by engineering geology focuses on distinguishing different types of inundation caused by the rise in water levels, while the concepts put forth by hydrogeology and environmental geology emphasize the description of changes in groundwater levels.

The occurrence of reservoir inundation often leads to the shoreline areas becoming submerged zones, which can cause extensive prime agricultural land to become marshy or salinized, decrease the foundation strength of buildings, roads, and railways, or even lead to overall instability. It may result in underground structures being flooded, the expansion and rupture of underground pipelines, or affect mineral extraction due to the flooding of mining pits. First, reservoir inundation aligns with the definitions of natural or anthropogenic hazards in the fields of disaster science and ecology, as it causes certain damages and hazards to both the natural ecological environment and human living conditions and human life and property. Therefore, reservoir inundation should be classified as a type of natural disaster. Second, classifying reservoir inundation as a disaster has two key advantages: ① It allows for research to be conducted on reservoir inundation disasters following the established methodologies in disaster science, systematically analyzing aspects such as disaster characteristics, disaster-causing factors, disaster patterns, affected areas, disaster losses, disaster management, and disaster risk reduction; ② Based on the dual nature of hazards, it allows for an objective analysis of the benefits and drawbacks brought by reservoir inundation to the impacted areas; ③ The classification of inundation hazards can attract greater public and scholarly attention, potentially promoting the development of this field of study. The following content studies the methods for assessing the affected areas of reservoir inundation disasters. Reservoir inundation is a complex disaster caused by the rise in surface water levels due to reservoir water storage or other human activities, leading to elevated shallow groundwater levels and triggering various problems (soil salinization, marshiness, reduced foundation strength of buildings, roads, railways, flooding or damage to underground structures and pipelines, etc.).

Based on the practical experience of reservoir inundation studies in the country, and considering the topography, geological structure of the reservoir banks, and the distribution and formation patterns of inundation areas, the modes of reservoir inundation can be categorized into four types: ① bank-type inundation, ② external inundation, ③ downstream inundation, ④ channel-type inundation. 

① Bank-type inundation (including river-channel inundation) is primarily distributed along the shoreline of the reservoir area. The inundation impact zone may be distributed in a narrow, elongated band, or due to reservoir siltation and riverbed elevation, it can cause extensive inundation on both banks. The groundwater level fluctuates with the reservoir water level, resulting in an unstable inundation range. The formation mechanism is mainly attributed to the rise in reservoir water level or the elevation of the backwater riverbed, which hampers groundwater drainage on both banks.

② External inundation (including low adjacent valleys and karst depressions) is primarily found in the areas surrounding low adjacent valleys and along the edges of sinkholes and the outlets of subterranean rivers. This type of inundation is relatively scattered and typically appears in a depression. The formation mechanism is mainly due to leakage from geomaterials, karst conduits, and fault zones in the inter-canyon blocks of the reservoir, resulting in inundation in low-lying areas or depressions. In some cases, the elevation of the reservoir water level can obstruct drainage at the subterranean river outlets and sinkholes, causing inundation in karst depressions.

③ Downstream inundation generally occurs in a patchy distribution but can also appear in a band along ancient riverbeds. Due to variations in topography, soil layer structure, and permeability properties, the characteristics of inundation distribution also differ. The formation mechanism is primarily caused by improper seepage control practices leading to leakage, resulting in extensive inundation downstream of the dam.

④ Channel-type inundation (including inundation caused by canals, surface rivers, and levee seepage) frequently occurs in the floodplain areas of basin rivers and in irrigation districts of inter-river blocks. It mainly appears on both sides of channels and in low-lying areas, forming patchy and band-like distributions. The formation is mainly due to seepage from the channels and inadequate infiltration management of return irrigation water, leading to a rise in groundwater levels. Coupled with high soil salinity and significant evaporation, this results in secondary salinization and marshland.

The formation mechanism of bank-side inundation in reservoirs is primarily due to the rise in water levels or the uplift of the riverbed at the reservoir's tail, resulting in poor drainage of groundwater along both banks. Its characteristics are threefold: First, the distribution of inundation zones occurs along the reservoir area and riverbanks; second, the morphology of the inundation impact area may be narrow and elongated, or it may result in large-scale inundation on one or both banks due to sedimentation in the reservoir and riverbed elevation; third, the extent of inundation is unstable as groundwater levels fluctuate with reservoir water levels. The occurrence of bank-side inundation in reservoirs is the result of various contributing factors, mainly involving the topography and landforms of the reservoir banks, lithology and structure, hydrogeological conditions, water chemistry, hydrometeorological factors, reservoir operational methods, and human activities. Moreover, the unique hydrological effects in cold regions further complicate the factors influencing reservoir inundation. Due to the complex nature of these factors and the interdisciplinary overlaps involving engineering geology, hydrogeology, environmental geology, and hydraulic structures, systematic analysis of bank-side inundation factors remains insufficient.

Common impacts of reservoir inundation include the following three categories: (1) Soil and crops: After reservoir inundation, excessive moisture in agricultural fields often leads to waterlogging and flooding disasters. Additionally, under evaporation, areas with shallow groundwater are prone to soil salinization and swamping. (2) Foundation of structures: Following reservoir inundation, the saturation and increased moisture content of the rocks and soils can deteriorate foundation conditions for buildings (houses, bridges, railways, roads). Furthermore, freeze-thaw effects in cold regions can lead to additional damage such as frost heave and liquefaction. (3) Underground structures: After reservoir inundation, the rising groundwater level often infiltrates underground structures, pipelines, and mines that lack adequate waterproofing, resulting in flooding and seepage. The impact of reservoir inundation on the aforementioned objects poses direct or indirect threats to life, production safety, and economic and environmental development. The conventional influencing factors of reservoir inundation include groundwater levels, soil texture (capillary rise height), as well as the water tolerance of surface crops and the foundations of structures (safety factors). Special influencing factors of reservoir inundation in cold regions should encompass the effects of soil freeze-thaw cycles and snowmelt on inundation. However, the freeze-thaw action on soil corresponds to the second conventional influencing factor regarding soil texture (capillary rise height and soil permeability), while snowmelt influences inundation by recharging groundwater levels. Therefore, this paper categorizes the analyzed influencing factors of reservoir inundation in cold regions into three principle types: (1) Groundwater levels under conventional and snowmelt conditions, (2) Soil texture under conventional and freeze-thaw conditions, and (3) Water tolerance of surface crops and foundations of structures.

Submergence is a typical geological issue in reservoir areas. The lessons learned from the environmental and ecological issues in surrounding areas caused by the rising groundwater level following the water storage of reservoirs have heightened awareness of the submergence problem. Yanqing New Town is located in the northwest of Beijing, situated in the eastern part of the Yanhuai Basin, adjacent to Guanting Reservoir, with the Guishui River flowing through the county from northeast to southwest. Influenced by the water storage of Guanting Reservoir, submergence disasters have previously occurred along both banks of the Guishui River in the New Town, resulting in marshland formation and partial building collapses, adversely affecting the lives of local residents and agricultural production. In recent years, due to drought conditions, the water level of Guanting Reservoir has been relatively low, leading to a year-on-year decline in regional groundwater levels, which has reduced the extent of the submerged area. However, considering the increasing number of high-rise constructions planned for the new urban area, where the foundation bearing layer primarily lies below the groundwater level, any significant rainfall could lead to substantial increases in reservoir water levels and elevated surrounding groundwater levels, posing potential hazards to the planning and construction of the New Town. Therefore, this paper conducts a systematic analysis and evaluation of the submergence issues in the planned area, which has significant theoretical significance and practical value, and the technical methods can also be applied to other similar research projects. Based on regional geological and meteorological hydrological data, the analysis identifies the development status of submergence in both Guanting Reservoir and the Yanqing New Town planning area, investigates the influencing factors of submergence, and focuses on studying the relationship between reservoir water level fluctuations and submergence disasters. Utilizing groundwater dynamics and Modflow numerical simulation methods, the analysis calculates the changes in groundwater levels in the study area, deriving groundwater elevation conditions under varying reservoir water levels to predict the extent and risk level of submergence in the study area. Finally, based on the characteristics and development trends of submergence in the study area, effective prevention and control measures are proposed. This includes an introduction to the fundamental principles and internal structure of Modflow calculations, an analysis of the model boundary setting principles, and the use of inverse methods to determine hydrogeological parameters, resulting in a good fit and offering a reference for the simulation predictions. Furthermore, the joint prediction study using both groundwater dynamics and Modflow numerical analysis methods yielded consistent results, demonstrating certain practical value.

After the reservoir is constructed and filled with water, the surrounding groundwater level is influenced by factors such as hydraulic pressure from the reservoir water and seepage, leading to an elevated water table. When the elevated groundwater level approaches or exceeds critical thresholds, it may result in ecological environmental degradation, such as land salinization, marsh formation, reduced agricultural yields, decreased foundation strength of buildings, and subsidence damage. This secondary geological hazard is referred to as inundation. Inundation issues arise alongside the construction of hydraulic projects. After the completion and filling of the Guanting Reservoir in 1954, inundation disasters occurred in the surrounding areas, causing severe damage. Subsequently, in 1957 and 1984, water conservancy departments and the former Ministry of Geology and Mineral Resources conducted investigations and analyses concerning the inundation problems of the Guanting Reservoir, which gradually drew public attention to these issues. In 1959, after the Huangbizhuang Reservoir in Luquan City, Hebei Province was completed and filled, rising water levels during the flood season led to inundation disasters in multiple areas downstream of the auxiliary dam, resulting in house collapses and marsh formation. Improvements were made through engineering measures such as the construction of drainage ditches. In 2000, after the Yanwang Bizi Reservoir in Chaoyang County, Liaoning Province was commissioned, on January 2001, when the water level reached 209.5 m, the nearby villages along the reservoir's edges experienced wall cracks and well collapses, attributed to the impacts of reservoir inundation disasters. Such inundation disasters have adverse effects on engineering construction, agricultural production, and other relevant sectors, seriously threatening the social stability of the reservoir area, necessitating focused investigation and predictive research.

Due to the impoundment of the reservoir, the stored water exerts a buoyant effect on the groundwater levels surrounding the reservoir area, leading to severe inundation hazards in the upstream regions, particularly along the banks of the Yongding River, Yang River, Sanggan River, and Guishui River. The inundation has caused widespread soil salinization and marsh formation in arable lands, resulting in reduced yields or even death of crops and fruit trees, damage to buildings and engineering facilities, and the forced relocation of villages, adversely affecting the livelihoods of local residents and severely constraining the socio-economic development of the region. In recent years, the extent of inundation in the reservoir area has visibly decreased, and the inundation hazards have shifted from severe to a state of mitigation. Currently, the soil salinization and marsh formation phenomena in the formerly inundated zones of Guanting Reservoir are no longer significant or have virtually disappeared, and no deterioration of structural stability has been observed in buildings due to adverse foundation conditions. Only minor inundation issues such as soil salinization and marsh formation remain in certain low-lying floodplains and reservoir banks. However, with changes in regional climate and potential increases in precipitation leading to substantial rises in reservoir water levels, there is a risk of heightened groundwater levels surrounding the reservoir, potentially expanding the inundation area and posing certain threats to agriculture and structures. Therefore, it is crucial to recognize and appropriately address these concerns.

The factors influencing inundation in the Yanqing Guanting Reservoir area are both internal and external. Internal factors primarily include geological conditions such as topography, landforms, stratigraphy, lithology, geological structures, and hydrogeological characteristics. External factors mainly comprise atmospheric precipitation, reservoir water levels, sedimentation in the reservoir, and agricultural water use practices. Internal factors are fundamental, while external factors serve as conditions; external factors exert influence through internal factors. 

The topographical and geomorphological conditions primarily consider the relationship between the ground elevation in the surrounding area of the reservoir and the reservoir water level, as well as the variations in surface undulations. The stratigraphy and lithology focus on the permeability of the soil and rock mass along the reservoir banks and the types of soil and rock in nearby areas. Geological structures play a crucial role in controlling groundwater movement, thereby affecting the occurrence of inundation. 

Hydrogeological conditions consider three main factors: 1) whether the groundwater in the study area is influenced by reservoir impoundment or river flow; 2) the drainage conditions for the groundwater; and 3) the conditions of groundwater occurrence. If the groundwater in the study area is shallow and poorly drained, prolonged reservoir impoundment can lead to groundwater accumulation, resulting in inundation issues. 

Climatic conditions are intricately linked to the severity of inundation disasters in the reservoir area, influencing reservoir water levels, groundwater dynamics, and soil moisture. The proportional relationship between precipitation and evaporation, alongside fluctuations in atmospheric moisture content and temperature during specific years, impacts the inundation process. In years of abundant rainfall, increased precipitation enhances vertical infiltration into surface water, which in turn raises reservoir levels, making inundation more likely. 

The operational water level of the reservoir is closely related to the dynamics of groundwater levels along the banks, making it a key factor influencing groundwater level fluctuations. Sedimentation in the Guanting Reservoir affects inundation in the Yanqing Basin primarily by raising water levels, causing sediment deposition to extend upwards in the Guishui River and its tributaries. This elevation of the riverbed leads to higher surface water drainage outlets along the riverbanks, resulting in poor drainage and internal flooding, which is a significant factor contributing to inundation in the river floodplain areas. 

Since the 1990s, the surface and groundwater resources in the Yanqing Basin have been diminishing, while the volume of water used for agricultural irrigation and residential purposes has been steadily increasing. In summary, inundation results from a combination of various factors. The primary causes of inundation disasters in this study area are the high water levels in the reservoir, the duration of those levels, and the amount of precipitation. When the reservoir operates at high water levels for extended periods, coupled with abundant atmospheric precipitation, inundation is more likely to occur.

Since the beginning of water storage at the Guanting Reservoir in 1954, flooding disasters such as land marsh formation and house collapses have occurred on both banks of the Nai Shui River. In 1957, the water conservancy authorities organized relevant units to investigate the flooding issues of the reservoir, identifying more severe inundation on the left bank around Dawangzhuang and Maying, as well as on the right bank in the areas of Xitun, Xiaohetun, Dongwuliying, and Tiansongying. Between 1984 and 1986, investigations and surveys regarding the inundation issues of the Guanting Reservoir were also conducted by the Ministry of Geology and Mineral Resources and other related units. The severely flooded area (groundwater depth ≤ 0.50m) is distributed along the reservoir's edge between Yaojiaying and Xiwuliying, while the moderately flooded area (groundwater depth 0.51~1.50m) is situated on the north side of Dawangzhuang. Lightly flooded areas (groundwater depth 1.51~3.0m) are extensively distributed around the reservoir. In the past two decades, Yanqing County has developed rapidly, expanding its urban area and witnessing a surge in high-rise buildings, with their foundations primarily situated below the groundwater level; moreover, Lianhua Lake has a water surface elevation of 479m, and there have been no significant flooding disasters in the current urban area, leading to the conclusion that there are no inundation issues in Yanqing County. 

From the analysis of groundwater depth, areas outside the flood zone (i.e., ground elevation above 479m) exhibit considerable groundwater depth, generally exceeding 3.0m, with areas less than 3.0m being relatively small in distribution. When the groundwater depth is greater than 3.0m, the current soil cultivation layer is in a dry state, indicating no flooding issues; no significant secondary salinization phenomena are observed in the areas where groundwater depth ranges between 2.0m and 3.0m, suggesting negligible flooding hazards; however, areas with groundwater depth less than 2.0m exhibit surface "tide" phenomena. The local saying “drought brings tidal soil” reflects the adverse effects of the groundwater depth in this range on crops, indicating flooding problems, particularly in low-lying areas between Dawangzhuang and Tiansongying, as well as near the reservoir's shoreline. 

Regarding the content of soluble salts in the soil, there is a slight decline compared to the survey results from 1984-1986. Soil analysis results from 1984-1986 indicated that the soluble salt content around the reservoir was generally less than 0.1%, with only one area in the southeast of Xiaoying Village reaching a soluble salt content of 534.06mg/100g soil, exceeding the salinized soil standard of 0.5% salt content. Currently, the soluble salt content usually ranges from 0.02% to 0.14%, with only the area of Tiansongying reaching 0.45%. Through the investigation, the current inundated area is 16.48km², with the area beyond the flood zone (ground elevation above 479m) covering 8.68km². It can be observed that the current flood extent has significantly decreased, with the severity of inundation shifting from severe to minor.

In the karst area of the Hongshui River mainstream, a significant number of karst underground river systems develop, with the Hongshui River often serving as the base level for drainage. During the annual flood season, due to local heavy rainfall (or torrential rain), the recharge of the karst underground river system increases, resulting in higher flow rates. Constrained by the cross-section of their drainage pathways (pipes), groundwater flows through surface karst depressions or valleys via skylights and/or sinkholes, inundating the farmland within these depressions or valleys, commonly referred to as internal flooding. At this time, the water level of the Hongshui River rises, submerging the outlets of the underground river system, further exacerbating the internal flooding. 

Reservoirs constructed on the Hongshui River elevate the river water level, thus raising the baseline for groundwater discharge to a fixed value. Some karst valleys or depressions located at the reservoir's shore, while not directly submerged by the reservoir, experience backwater effects from the reservoir water traveling through karst channels (such as pipes or fissures), leading to a reduction in the hydraulic gradient of the karst underground river system, decreased groundwater velocity, and diminished drainage capacity. Consequently, the internal water level rises, extending and intensifying the internal flooding in areas previously affected by flooding, while causing flooding phenomena in areas that were originally unaffected, referred to as internal flooding due to reservoir-induced karst inundation.

The karst inundation and internal flooding disaster in the karst area is a natural and/or anthropogenic phenomenon resulting from the redistribution of internal water volume within a multiple medium environment during local heavy rainfall (or torrential rain). It is determined by factors from both "heaven" and "earth." "Heaven" refers to the presence of rainstorm clouds containing large amounts of water vapor in the local atmosphere and the dynamic conditions that facilitate the condensation of this vapor; "earth" pertains to the integrity and complexity of the surface and subsurface environments in the karst area, including the existence of karst underground river systems and the presence of numerous interconnected karst depressions and valleys.

The inundation and flooding disaster in karst areas is a marginal disaster type occurring at the intersection of meteorological hazards, geological hazards, and anthropogenic hazards. The primary geological factors influencing the occurrence and development of inundation and flooding disasters in karst areas are as follows: 

1. **Karst Surface Structure**: In the main stream karst area of the Hongshui River, the karst surface morphological combinations include peak cluster depressions, peak cluster valleys, peak forest valleys, peak forest plains, and isolated peak plains. The characteristics of the karst landform (such as surface elevation, slope, and cutting density) directly affect the drainage and collection conditions of surface water flow. Due to the concave relief of the karst surface, which primarily consists of closed or semi-closed depressions and valleys, the water collection function significantly outweighs the drainage function, leading to a lack or incompleteness in the surface drainage system. This is a key factor for the frequent occurrence of inundation and flooding disasters during heavy rainfall (or torrential rain).

2. **Karst Underground Structure**: The objective presence of five types of water-bearing medium void spaces—cavities, caves, fractures, gaps, and conduits—within the karst underground structure, along with its diverse configuration forms, especially the development and evolution of the karst underground river system and its stratification and inheritance, are major determinants of the occurrence and development of karst inundation and flooding disasters. The existence of the karst underground river system enhances the function of the drainage collection system in the underground karst structure. When the water collection capacity of the karst underground river system is greater than or equal to its drainage capacity, inundation and flooding disasters are typically triggered during heavy rainfall (or torrential rain) events.

3. **Multi-layered Composite Structure of the Karst Surface and Underground**: The karst region in the main stream of the Hongshui River exhibits a harmonious composite form of surface and underground karst development, with alternating and mixed old and new forms that communicate with each other, making them difficult to distinguish. The combinations are complex and varied, including peak cluster mountains, valleys, peak forest plains, low-lying areas, deep and shallow depressions, sinkholes, vertical shafts, dissolution ponds, skylights, solution ditches, solution pans, foot caves, and numerous stratified caves; while underground consists of multilayered and multiple combinations of conduits, caves, fractures, gaps, and voids, forming specific water transmission and storage spaces, such as underground river channels and lakes. The connection and functional arrangement between the karst surface and underground structures have unique configuration modes in both time and space, which is a critical factor in determining the occurrence of karst inundation and flooding disasters.

4. **Temporal and Spatial Effects of "Three Waters" Transformation**: The coupling and connectivity between the karst surface and underground structures lead to the rapid and variable conversion of atmospheric precipitation, surface water, and groundwater over time, with complex and diverse spatial patterns. This transformation is a major factor determining the occurrence of karst inundation and flooding disasters. Additionally, anthropogenic geological processes also play an important role in the formation of karst inundation and flooding disasters, primarily manifested in two aspects: first, certain human economic activities (such as mining, engineering excavation, land leveling for agriculture, and urban-rural construction) create disaster conditions through anthropogenic processes of transport, accumulation, shaping, and erosion; second, the rational and irrational water conservancy projects shape the artificial environment, laying the foundation for disaster causation.

Before the reservoir project, the water level in the dry season at the Yaobao platform section of the Songhua River main stream generally falls below 113 m. The main flood season begins on June 15 and ends at the end of September each year. After the reservoir project is completed, the environmental conditions of the river water facing the levees will change from a flood control project that only functions during the flood season to a year-round water-affected reservoir embankment project. The levees will be constantly saturated by river water, and 12 km of the levee slope and riverbank have experienced collapses and slope failures due to not implementing concrete prefabricated block protection where the toe elevation exceeds 116 m, thereby endangering levee safety. Following the filling of the reservoir, the water level of the Songhua River has risen, causing river water to seep into the levee foundation, raising the groundwater level in the flood-prone area adjacent to the river, which is separated by only the levee. As a result, surface water infiltration into the flood-prone area has significantly decreased. Additionally, the insufficient drainage channels in the flood-prone area and the sediment accumulation in the existing drainage system have led to poor drainage over many years, preventing the drainage stations from operating at full capacity and obstructing rapid drainage, resulting in surface water accumulation and floods in the area. This has subsequently caused secondary disasters, such as land salinization and swamp formation. Elevated groundwater levels can raise capillary water, and when the height reaches the foundation of buildings or the root systems of crops and trees, sustained for a long duration, it can lead to inundation issues. Inundation can reduce agricultural crop yields, deteriorate foundation conditions of industrial, mining, and civilian buildings, increase inflow in mines, and cause upheaval and frost heave on railways and roads. The inundation of reservoirs is a significant environmental geological issue that must not be ignored, as demonstrated by numerous cases throughout the country.

After the reservoir is filled, the rock and soil body along the reservoir bank becomes gradually saturated due to water infiltration, resulting in an increase in groundwater levels and the formation of backwater conditions. If the slope is relatively gentle and the groundwater level approaches or exceeds the ground surface, this leads to a reduction in the strength of the bank's rock and soil body, causing large areas of land to become marshy or severely salinized. This phenomenon is referred to as reservoir inundation. Following the rise in water levels within the reservoir, changes occur in the surrounding groundwater levels. If the geological conditions around the reservoir hinder underground drainage or if the groundwater levels were initially very low prior to reservoir construction, the water table may rise significantly, potentially exceeding the surface elevation. This may result in issues such as water infiltrating building foundations, surface water emergence, agricultural land salinization, and increased humidity in residential areas. The problem of reservoir inundation essentially represents a series of issues triggered by the rising water table. According to regulations, inundation along the reservoir bank occurs only in areas with gentle topography composed of soil. As the reservoir fills, the rise in groundwater levels along the bank leads to an increase in capillary water levels within the soil layers, resulting in surface soil becoming marshy, salt-affected, and causing cold flooding in rice fields along the southern bank, which can lead to crop failures or weaken the foundation, posing safety risks to structures.

Effects of Reservoir Inundation

Reservoir inundation primarily impacts construction, farmland, living environments, and ecological and geological aspects.

(1) Impact on Construction  
Inundation causes the foundations of buildings near the reservoir bank to become saturated, leading to a reduction in strength. Additionally, the rise in groundwater levels and corresponding increase in capillary water can result in damp walls, leading to peeling paint and potential structural failures.

(2) Impact on Farmland  
Inundation affects farmland in two main ways: first, the rise in groundwater levels can lead to land becoming marshy and saline-alkaline; second, the submersion of paddy fields can result in reduced crop yields.

(3) Impact on Living Environment  
The effects of inundation on living conditions are mainly seen in persistently humid air, water pooling in low-lying areas, and drainage difficulties during rainfall. Prolonged exposure to a humid environment can lead to discomfort for residents.

(4) Impact on Ecology  
The impact of inundation on the ecological environment primarily leads to the formation of saline-alkaline land and marshlands. Occasionally, this can benefit the local environment; for instance, after the water storage in the Sanmenxia Reservoir, several wetlands formed in Henan Province. However, if the reservoir's water storage function ceases, these wetlands will shrink and disappear, adversely affecting residential areas or farmland.

(5) Impact on Geological Conditions  
Inundation mainly affects geological conditions by altering soil characteristics. Inundation causes soil (especially loess) to weaken, reducing its load-bearing capacity. Fluctuations in water levels on soil slopes can lead to slope instability, which may result in the collapse of water wells in residential areas. In northern loess regions, occurrences of landslides and bank collapses may increase in frequency due to inundation.
